care.summary: Snake Plant (Dracaena trifasciata, formerly Sansevieria trifasciata) is one of the hardiest houseplants — thrives on neglect with tall, upright sword-like leaves featuring yellow edges and green cross-banding. Excellent for low-light corners and office desks.
care.light: Low to bright indirect light. Tolerates low-light conditions better than most plants. Avoid direct hot sun which can scorch leaf tips. Fluorescent office light is sufficient.
care.water: Water sparingly every 2-6 weeks depending on light and season. Let soil dry completely between waterings. In winter, water only once a month or less. Overwatering is the #1 cause of death.
care.soil: Fast-draining cactus or succulent potting mix. Add perlite or coarse sand for extra drainage. Avoid heavy potting soil that retains moisture.
care.humidity: Very low to medium humidity (20-50%). Thrives in dry indoor air and bathrooms with minimal humidity. No misting required.
care.fertilizer: Feed with diluted succulent or balanced fertilizer once in spring and once in summer. Do not fertilize in winter. Over-fertilizing causes leaf droop.
care.toxicity: Mildly toxic if ingested — contains saponins that cause nausea, vomiting, and drooling in pets and humans. Keep out of reach of cats and dogs.
common_issues: Root rot from overwatering — mushy, yellowing leaves at base; reduce watering and repot in dry soil
Brown leaf tips — fluoride in tap water or dry air; use filtered water and wipe leaves
Mealybugs — white cotton-like clusters at leaf base; wipe with rubbing alcohol
Leaf splitting or curling — underwatering or physical damage; increase watering slightly
Fungal leaf spot — dark spots with yellow halos; improve air circulation and avoid wetting leaves
Etiolation (leggy, pale growth) — insufficient light; move to brighter spot gradually
tips: Use a terracotta pot with drainage holes — helps wick excess moisture away from roots
Wipe leaves with a damp cloth monthly to remove dust and keep stomata clear
Propagate easily by division (separate pups/rhizomes) or leaf cuttings in water or soil
Rotate pot quarterly for even growth — leaves grow toward light source
Snake plants are CAM plants — they release oxygen at night, making them ideal bedroom plants
Leaves can be trimmed with clean scissors if tips get damaged; cut at an angle to mimic natural shape
Let cuttings callous over for 2-3 days before placing in water to prevent rot
